name: SpiderFoot
description: SpiderFoot automates OSINT for threat intelligence and mapping your attack surface.
keyFeatures: Automates OSINT collection by integrating with a huge range of data sources and modules
Built-in web-based UI for running scans and visually navigating results
Maps attack surface and gathers threat intelligence for targets (domains, IPs, emails, etc.)
Usable as a standalone tool or embedded via its API/CLI for automation workflows

name: theHarvester
description: E-mails, subdomains and names Harvester - OSINT tool for gathering names, emails, IPs, subdomains, and URLs during the reconnaissance phase of red team assessments and penetration tests.
keyFeatures: Aggregates OSINT data (emails, subdomains, IPs, names, URLs) on a target domain using dozens of passive modules (e.g. Baidu, Censys, crt.sh, Shodan)
Designed for the reconnaissance stage of red team engagements and penetration tests
Installable via uv/pip with an active Python (3.12+) codebase and CI pipeline
Optional REST API routes protected via THEHARVESTER_API_KEY header auth

name: Amass
description: In-depth attack surface mapping and asset discovery
keyFeatures: Network mapping of attack surfaces via OSINT and active reconnaissance
External asset discovery and subdomain enumeration
OWASP flagship project with active Discord community and Docker image
Extensive data source integrations for footprinting

name: Photon
description: Incredibly fast crawler designed for OSINT.
keyFeatures: Extracts URLs, emails, social media accounts, secret keys/API keys, JS endpoints, subdomains and DNS data while crawling
Supports custom regex pattern matching and exporting results as organized/JSON output
Flexible crawl controls: timeout, delay, seed URLs, URL exclusion patterns
Plugin support including Wayback Machine seeding and dnsdumpster DNS data dumping

name: BBOT
description: The recursive internet scanner for hackers — a multipurpose OSINT/recon tool inspired by Spiderfoot, built to automate reconnaissance, bug bounties, and attack surface management (ASM).
keyFeatures: Recursive scanning engine that chains 90+ modules together for subdomain enumeration, port scanning, web screenshots, and more
Finds 20-50% more subdomains than comparable tools via passive API sources plus target-specific DNS brute-forcing
Modular, flag-based preset system (e.g. subdomain-enum) for customizing scan scope and output
Output integrations and visualizations (e.g. VivaGraphJS) for real-time scan graphing

name: recon-ng
description: Open Source Intelligence gathering tool aimed at reducing the time spent harvesting information from open sources.
keyFeatures: Full-featured OSINT reconnaissance framework with a Metasploit-like modular CLI interface
Marketplace-style module installation for web-based, API-based, and dorking search modules
Independent database interaction for storing, exporting, and correlating harvested recon data
Built-in reporting engine and interactive help system for building custom collection workflows

name: Sherlock
description: Hunt down social media accounts by username across social networks
keyFeatures: Searches for a given username across a large number of social media and web platforms
Command-line tool written in Python for OSINT and username enumeration
Reports which platforms have a matching account for the target username
Actively maintained with a dedicated project site (sherlockproject.xyz)

name: Maigret
description: Collect a dossier on a person by username from 3000+ sites
keyFeatures: Searches 3000+ sites for a given username to build a person dossier
Generates HTML/PDF/JSON reports with discovered profiles and extracted metadata
Recursive search that extracts data (emails, links, other usernames) from found pages to expand the search
CLI, Docker, and API/web interface usage

name: GHunt
description: Offensive Google framework, currently focused on OSINT, for gathering information on Google accounts (Gmail/Gaia), Drive files, and more.
keyFeatures: Get detailed info on an email address (Gaia ID, Google account details) or a Gaia ID directly
Investigate Google Drive files and folders for exposed metadata
Geolocate BSSIDs and find assets via Digital Asset Links (spiderdal)
CLI and Python library usage with JSON export and a browser companion extension for login

name: PhoneInfoga
description: Information gathering framework for phone numbers
keyFeatures: Gathers information on phone numbers using OSINT techniques (carrier, line type, country)
Cross-references numbers against search engines and social media/footprint sources
Modular scanner architecture (local, numverify, googlesearch, etc.)
Ships as a CLI tool and a web client/API for interactive investigations

name: holehe
description: Checks if an email address is registered on various websites (Twitter, Instagram, etc.) by exploiting forgotten-password functionality.
keyFeatures: Checks an email against 120+ sites/services for account registration
Exploits 'forgot password' flows to detect account existence without alerting the target
CLI tool usable in OSINT investigations and email verification workflows
Modular architecture allowing easy addition of new site modules

name: h8mail
description: Email OSINT & Password breach hunting tool, locally or using premium services. Supports chasing down related email
keyFeatures: Queries multiple breach/recon APIs (HaveIBeenPwned, Hunter.io, and more) plus local breach compilations like Collection#1
Email pattern matching via regex and can extract emails directly from URLs/pages
Chases and regroups related emails across targets, with bulk file input support
Outputs results to CSV or JSON, installable via pip

name: WhatsMyName
description: Community-maintained dataset of 700+ websites for finding accounts by username — powers OSINT and digital footprint tools.
keyFeatures: JSON dataset (wmn-data.json) of hundreds of sites for username existence checks, built to minimize false positives
Created in 2015 by Micah 'WebBreacher' Hoffman, now a widely-used community-maintained OSINT dataset
Powers numerous third-party username enumeration tools and web search front-ends
Actively maintained via community contributions with a documented contribution process

name: nuclei
description: A fast, customizable vulnerability scanner powered by the global security community and built on a simple YAML-based DSL, enabling collaboration to tackle trending vulnerabilities on the internet. It helps find vulnerabilities in applications, APIs, networks, DNS, and cloud configurations.
keyFeatures: YAML-based DSL for defining vulnerability and misconfiguration templates
Community-powered template library covering trending CVEs and exposures
Scans across applications, APIs, networks, DNS, and cloud configurations
Fast, highly customizable scanning engine written in Go

name: masscan
description: TCP port scanner that spews SYN packets asynchronously, capable of scanning the entire Internet in under 5 minutes.
keyFeatures: Asynchronous, stateless SYN-scanning engine for extremely high-speed port scans across huge IP ranges
Can scan the entire public IPv4 Internet in under 5 minutes at full rate
Supports flexible target/port ranges with nmap-like CLI syntax and output formats (XML, JSON, binary)
Includes banner-checking and adjustable transmission rate control to manage scan speed and bandwidth

name: RustScan
description: The Modern Port Scanner — fast, smart, effective.
keyFeatures: Scans all 65k ports in about 3 seconds
Full scripting engine support (Python, Lua, Shell) to pipe results into Nmap or custom scripts
Adaptive learning that improves scan performance the more it's used
Supports IPv6, CIDR ranges, file input, and Docker usage

name: katana
description: A next-generation crawling and spidering framework.
keyFeatures: Fast, fully configurable web crawling with standard and headless (JS-rendering) modes
Customizable automatic form filling and scope control via preconfigured fields or regex
ML-based knowledge base for page-type/form classification
Flexible input (STDIN, URL, list) and output (STDOUT, file, JSON) handling

name: subfinder
description: Fast passive subdomain enumeration tool.
keyFeatures: Fast and powerful resolution and wildcard elimination modules
Curated passive sources to maximize subdomain discovery results
Multiple output formats supported (JSON, file, stdout)
STDIN/STDOUT support for easy pipeline/workflow integration

name: Nmap
description: Nmap - the Network Mapper. GitHub mirror of the official SVN repository for the free and open-source network discovery and security auditing tool.
keyFeatures: Host discovery and port scanning across large networks
Service/version detection and OS fingerprinting
Scriptable interaction via the Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)
Includes Zenmap GUI front-end for visual scan management

name: httpx
description: httpx is a fast and multi-purpose HTTP toolkit that allows running multiple probes using the retryablehttp library.
keyFeatures: Fast, multi-purpose HTTP probing toolkit with a simple, modular codebase
Supports hosts, URLs, and CIDR ranges as input with high-concurrency threading
Smart HTTPS-to-HTTP auto fallback plus retry/backoff handling for WAFs
Wide range of probes: status code, title, TLS cert, CNAME, tech/CDN detection, screenshots, and more

name: naabu
description: A fast port scanner written in go with a focus on reliability and simplicity. Designed to be used in combination with other tools for attack surface discovery in bug bounties and pentests
keyFeatures: Fast SYN/CONNECT/UDP probe-based port scanning with automatic IP deduplication for DNS scans
IPv4/IPv6 support plus passive port enumeration via Shodan InternetDB
Nmap integration for service discovery and CDN/WAF exclusion
Multiple input (STDIN/host/IP/CIDR/ASN) and output formats (JSON/TXT/STDOUT)

name: OpenCTI
description: Open Cyber Threat Intelligence Platform
keyFeatures: Structures, stores, organizes and visualizes technical and non-technical cyber threat intelligence data
Knowledge schema based on the STIX2 standard with a GraphQL API and modern web frontend
Integrates with tools like MISP, TheHive, and the MITRE ATT&CK framework via dedicated connectors
Supports data import/export in multiple formats (CSV, STIX2 bundles) and inferred relationship analysis

name: MISP
description: MISP (core software) - Open Source Threat Intelligence and Sharing Platform
keyFeatures: Open source threat intelligence platform for gathering, storing, and sharing IOCs and threat data
Enables collaborative sharing communities and automated correlation between events/attributes
Supports structured data exports (STIX, OpenIOC, CSV) and integrations via API for SOC/CERT workflows
Includes taxonomy, galaxy, and warning-list frameworks for structured threat classification

name: OpenCVE
description: Vulnerability Intelligence Platform
keyFeatures: Aggregates CVEs from multiple sources (MITRE, NVD, RedHat, Vulnrichment) with filtering by vendor, product, CVSS, KEV, EPSS, CWE, and publication date
Subscribe to vendors/products and organize monitoring via projects, organizations, and reusable saved Views
Tag and prioritize CVEs, assign users/statuses to track analysis progress, with instant email or webhook alerts
Tracks full change history of CVEs for reporting and collaboration

name: ATT&CK Navigator
description: Web app that provides basic navigation and annotation of ATT&CK matrices
keyFeatures: Interactive visualization and annotation of MITRE ATT&CK matrices (color coding, comments, numerical scores per technique)
Custom "layers" - tailored views of the ATT&CK knowledge base, created interactively or generated programmatically
Supports all ATT&CK domains (Enterprise, Mobile, ICS) in a single instance since v4.0, plus older ATT&CK versions
Hosted live via GitHub Pages, and runnable as a self-hosted or local client-side-only application

name: Yeti
description: Your Everyday Threat Intelligence — a platform bridging CTI and DFIR by centralizing observables, TTPs, and threat data for analysts and incident responders.
keyFeatures: Bulk search and enrichment of observables (IOCs) with automatic threat context
Links threats to TTPs, malware, campaigns, and DFIR artifacts (Yara, Sigma, DFIQ)
Web API for automating queries and integrating with SIEM/DFIR/sandbox tooling
Supports custom data sources, analytics, and export formats for third-party ingestion

name: OpenTAXII
description: TAXII server implementation in Python from EclecticIQ
keyFeatures: Robust Python implementation of TAXII (Trusted Automated Exchange of Indicator Information) Services with a pythonic API
Guaranteed compatibility with Cabby, the TAXII client library
Ships as a Docker image (eclecticiq/opentaxii) for quick deployment with SQLite-backed services/collections/accounts
Feature-complete and stable; maintained for bug fixes with documentation on ReadTheDocs

name: Ghidra
description: Ghidra is a software reverse engineering (SRE) framework
keyFeatures: Full-featured SRE suite with disassembly, assembly, decompilation, graphing, and scripting
Supports a wide variety of processor instruction sets and executable formats across Windows, macOS, and Linux
Runs in both interactive and automated/headless modes for large-scale analysis
Extensible via custom Ghidra extensions and scripts written in Java or Python

name: YARA
description: The pattern matching swiss knife for malware researchers (and everyone else)
keyFeatures: Rule-based pattern matching engine using textual or binary string signatures to identify and classify malware families
Rules support wildcards, case-insensitive strings, regular expressions, and boolean condition logic
Multi-platform (Windows, Linux, macOS), usable via CLI or embedded in Python scripts through yara-python
Widely adopted industry-standard tool, now in maintenance mode as its successor YARA-X is developed

name: capa
description: The FLARE team's open-source tool to identify capabilities in executable files.
keyFeatures: Identifies capabilities (behaviors) in executable files such as PE, ELF, shellcode, and .NET binaries
Uses a human-readable rule format (like Yara for behaviors) to detect functionality without executing the sample
Developed by Mandiant's FLARE reverse engineering team for malware triage and analysis
Extensible rule set and library usable standalone or integrated into other analysis tools

name: Volatility 3
description: Volatility 3: the volatile memory extraction framework — a complete rewrite of the world's most widely used memory forensics tool for extracting digital artifacts from RAM samples.
keyFeatures: Extracts forensic artifacts from volatile memory (RAM) images independent of the running system
Complete architectural rewrite of Volatility 2 for improved performance and maintainability
Plugin-based framework (e.g. windows.info) supporting Windows, macOS, and Linux memory samples
Uses downloadable symbol table packs for accurate OS-specific memory structure parsing

name: rizin
description: UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset.
keyFeatures: Reverse engineering framework forked from radare2 with focus on usability and cleanliness
Disassembles, debugs, and analyzes binaries across a huge range of architectures (x86, ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, PowerPC, and dozens more)
Supports a wide array of executable/file formats (ELF, Mach-O, PE, DEX, WASM, coredumps, minidumps, etc.)
Scriptable command-line hex editor usable for forensic analysis of disk files and binaries

name: CAPEv2
description: Malware Configuration And Payload Extraction
keyFeatures: Malware sandbox derived from Cuckoo, executing malicious files in an isolated instrumented environment
Automated dynamic malware unpacking with YARA-based malware classification
Static and dynamic malware configuration/payload extraction
Automated debugger programmable via YARA signatures for custom unpacking, anti-sandbox countermeasures, and instruction traces

name: Atomic Red Team
description: Small and highly portable detection tests based on MITRE's ATT&CK.
keyFeatures: Library of over 1,800 atomic tests mapped directly to MITRE ATT&CK techniques
Tests can be executed straight from the command line with no installation required
Pairs with the Invoke-Atomic execution framework for more robust, repeatable testing
Community-developed and open source, with active Slack community and contribution guide

name: Sigma
description: Main Sigma Rule Repository — a generic, open signature format for describing log events, enabling detection engineers and threat hunters to write and share SIEM-agnostic detection rules.
keyFeatures: Over 3000 community-maintained detection rules covering generic, threat-hunting, emerging-threat, compliance, and placeholder use cases
Vendor-agnostic rule format convertible to many SIEM/query languages via tools like pySigma/sigma-cli
Organized rule categories: Generic Detection, Threat Hunting, Emerging Threats, and Compliance (CIS, NIST, ISO 27001)
Rules released under the Detection Rule License (DRL) 1.1; the Sigma spec itself is public domain

name: Chainsaw
description: Rapidly Search and Hunt through Windows Forensic Artefacts
keyFeatures: Hunt for threats using Sigma detection rules and custom Chainsaw detection rules
Search and extract forensic artefacts (Event Logs, MFT) via string matching and regex
Create execution timelines from Shimcache/Amcache and analyse the SRUM database
Fast, lightweight, written in Rust; outputs to ASCII table, CSV, or JSON; runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux

name: Hayabusa
description: Hayabusa is a sigma-based threat hunting and fast forensics timeline generator for Windows event logs.
keyFeatures: Sigma-based threat hunting with full Sigma v2 correlation rule support
Fast forensics timeline generation from Windows event logs
Written in memory-safe Rust for performance and safety
Actively maintained by Yamato Security with frequent releases

name: signature-base
description: YARA signature and IOC database for the LOKI and THOR Lite scanners, maintained by Florian Roth (Neo23x0).
keyFeatures: Large curated collection of high-quality YARA rules and IOCs designed for minimal false positives
Includes IOC files (CSV), YARA rules, and a Threat Intel API receiver for MISP and OTX
Powers the LOKI and THOR Lite IOC/YARA scanners
Licensed under the Detection Rule License (DRL) 1.1 as of Aug 2021, not GitHub-recognized SPDX/OSI license

name: detection-rules
description: Home for rules used by Elastic Security's Detection Engine — the repository for development, maintenance, testing, validation, and release of detection rules, plus threat hunting queries and Detections-as-Code tooling.
keyFeatures: Curated library of detection rules for Elastic Security's Detection Engine
Python tooling for rule parsing, validation, and packaging (detection_rules module)
Detections-as-Code (DaC) pipeline support with unit testing and Kibana API integration
Threat hunting query package (hunting/) and KQL parsing library

name: GRR Rapid Response
description: GRR Rapid Response: remote live forensics for incident response
keyFeatures: Python client (agent) deployed on target endpoints for remote live forensics
Python server infrastructure to manage and communicate with many clients at scale
Supports incident response workflows like filesystem/endpoint inspection and results review
Open-source framework maintained by Google for large-scale endpoint investigation

name: Velociraptor
description: Digging Deeper.... An advanced digital forensic and incident response (DFIR) / endpoint visibility and collection tool.
keyFeatures: Endpoint visibility and evidence collection using the Velociraptor Query Language (VQL)
Built-in GUI, frontend, and client for interactive or fleet-wide artifact collection
Self-contained local triage collectors buildable directly from the GUI
Server-side deployment options including Docker for scaled incident response

name: TheHive
description: Collaborative Security Incident Response and Case Management Platform. Note: repo is archived — versions 3.x/4.x are end-of-life (no longer distributed publicly since 2023), and TheHive is now offered only as a commercial product by StrangeBee.
keyFeatures: Collaborative case and alert management for SOC/CSIRT/CERT teams
Originally MISP/Cortex-integrated for threat intel enrichment and observable analysis
Repository archived; 3.x/4.x no longer maintained or downloadable since 2023
Successor is a closed-source commercial product from StrangeBee

name: Timesketch
description: Collaborative forensic timeline analysis
keyFeatures: Collaborative, web-based timeline analysis for digital forensics and incident response
Enables multiple analysts to work together on the same timeline/case simultaneously
Built on Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for fast search and filtering across large event timelines
Supports timesketch API/CLI and integrates with tools like Plaso for forensic artifact ingestion

name: Cortex
description: Cortex: a Powerful Observable Analysis and Active Response Engine
keyFeatures: Analyzes observables (IPs, URLs, hashes, domains, emails) one-by-one or in bulk via a web UI
Automatable through a full REST API for integration into SOC/IR workflows
Pluggable analyzer ecosystem (cortex-analyzers) for threat intel and active response actions
Designed as the natural companion to TheHive and MISP for case management and threat intel sharing

name: DFIR IRIS
description: Collaborative Incident Response platform
keyFeatures: Web collaborative platform for incident responders to share technical details during investigations
Modular architecture: IrisWeb core plus IrisModules extensions (e.g., IOC enrichment via MISP/VirusTotal, EVTX injection into Splunk)
Dockerized deployment (app, PostgreSQL DB, RabbitMQ, worker, NGINX) for quick setup and upgrades
Configurable via Azure Key Vault, environment variables, or configuration files

name: CyberChef
description: The Cyber Swiss Army Knife - a web app for encryption, encoding, compression and data analysis
keyFeatures: Encoding/decoding operations (Base64, XOR, hex/binary dumps) and encryption/decryption (AES, DES, Blowfish)
Data compression, decompression, and hashing/checksum calculation
IPv6 and X.509 certificate parsing plus character encoding conversion
Drag-and-drop recipe builder combining chained operations in the browser

name: IntelOwl
description: IntelOwl: manage your Threat Intelligence at scale
keyFeatures: Aggregates dozens of online analyzers and malware analysis tools behind a single API request for IPs, domains, URLs, hashes and files
Built to scale out and speed up threat intelligence collection with a pluggable analyzer/connector architecture
Pyintelowl client and web UI for orchestrating enrichment jobs and visualizing results
Docker-based deployment with active CI/CD, CodeQL, and OpenSSF Scorecard security tooling

name: msticpy
description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence Security Tools
keyFeatures: Query log data from multiple sources (Sentinel, Splunk, Defender for Endpoint, Microsoft Graph, etc.)
Enrich data with Threat Intelligence feeds, geolocation, and Azure resource data
Extract Indicators of Activity (IoA) from logs and unpack encoded data
Visualize data via interactive timelines, process trees, and multi-dimensional Morph Charts

name: IntelMQ
description: A solution for IT security teams (CERTs/CSIRTs) for collecting and processing security threat intelligence feeds using a message queuing protocol.
keyFeatures: Message-queue based pipeline for collecting, parsing, and processing security feeds
Modular bots architecture with collectors, parsers, experts (enrichment) and outputs
Harmonized data format for normalizing events across many feed sources
Built for CERT/CSIRT incident response and threat intel automation workflows

name: ThreatIngestor
description: Extract and aggregate threat intelligence.
keyFeatures: Extracts IOCs (IPs, domains, YARA signatures) from threat feeds like Twitter, RSS, and sitemap/XML sources
Plugin architecture with pluggable source (input) and operator (output) modules
Integrates out-of-the-box with ThreatKB, MISP, AWS SQS, and Beanstalk for existing workflows
Runs continuously, polling configured sources on a set interval (default 15 minutes)

name: PyMISP
description: Python library to access MISP platforms via their REST API, for fetching, adding, and updating threat intelligence events and attributes.
keyFeatures: Fetch, add, and update MISP events and attributes via REST API
Create structured MISP objects (PE/ELF/Mach-O file objects, email objects, URL objects)
Optional integrations for VirusTotal enrichment, OpenIOC import, and PDF report generation
Supports import/export workflows for threat intel feeds and orchestration

name: STIX-shifter
description: Open source Python library that connects to disparate security data repositories using STIX Patterning and returns results as normalized STIX Observations.
keyFeatures: Translates STIX 2 Patterns into native queries for products like SIEMs, EDR, threat intel platforms, and data lakes
Transforms native query results into normalized STIX 2 Observations for consistent cross-source analysis
Modular connector architecture (stix-shifter-modules-<name>) supporting many data source integrations (e.g. QRadar)
Provides a bundled command-line utility for pattern translation and query transmission
